label: tetlock-est-Q3
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**Does directing a majority (55%) of surcharge revenue to rebates and programs that predominantly benefit customers other than the surcharge-payers violate §6(b)(2)'s requirement that fee revenues 'shall not exceed the funds required to provide the property related service,' or §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement — i.e., is cross-subsidization from high-tier payers to non-payer rebate recipients categorically impermissible under Prop 218?**

What would have to be true: Would need to determine: (1) whether case law requires revenue from a property-related fee to be spent on services provided to the payor class specifically, or whether district-wide conservation benefit (shared aquifer/supply reliability) satisfies the nexus even if direct rebates flow elsewhere; (2) how courts have treated similarly structured 'polluter pays into a mitigation fund for others' fee designs; (3) whether the debt-service (30%) and ambassador-staff (15%) allocations are more defensible than the 55% rebate allocation because they arguably serve system-wide supply/conservation service to all ratepayers including payors.
text: {"probability":55,"key_considerations":["Numbering note: the 'revenue shall not exceed funds required' language is actually Art. XIII D §6(b)(1), not (b)(2); §6(b)(3) is the parcel-level proportionality clause. This matters for how a complaint would plead the claim, though the substantive analysis is similar either way.","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 is the closest direct precedent: it held tiered rates must be justified by actual marginal/incremental cost of service, and rejected 'conservation-incentive' pricing untethered from cost as a standalone justification. The district's own admission that $0.85 derives partly from incentive pricing (not strictly the 1.6x cost differential) is the strongest evidence for a violation — but only as to the increment above cost, not necessarily the whole surcharge.","No CA case squarely holds that funding conservation rebates/education from rate revenue is a per se or 'categorical' Prop 218 violation; turf/appliance rebate programs funded from tiered rates are common utility practice and are often defensible as system-wide demand-management costs (reduced peaking/capacity needs benefit all ratepayers, including payors), which cuts against a bright-line rule.","City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191 and Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 support a 'reasonable relationship'/no-mathematical-precision standard, giving agencies some latitude to pool revenue across an enterprise fund for legitimate service-related purposes without dollar-for-dollar payor traceability — suggesting courts may tolerate the 30% debt-service and 15% education allocations more readily than the 55% rebate allocation.","A court is more likely to sever the analysis (invalidating only the incentive-pricing markup or the rebate-heavy 55% tranche) than to adopt the sweeping 'categorically impermissible' framing in the sub-question itself; the doctrine is fact/cost-methodology-driven, not a per se rule, which should modestly lower confidence in any absolute answer either direction."],"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Expert/actuarial recalculation showing how much of the $0.85 rate is attributable to the 1.6x cost differential versus pure incentive pricing (a small markup favors the district; a large one favors plaintiffs); the rate study's own characterization of rebate/education/conservation program costs as part of 'cost of service' to high-tier users specifically (would reframe the allocation as internally cost-justified rather than cross-subsidized); and any post-Capistrano published appellate decisions specifically addressing rebate-funded tiered surcharges (e.g., subsequent water-rate Prop 218 litigation) that either extend or cabin Capistrano's reasoning to revenue-use questions rather than just rate-derivation questions."}

key_considerations: Numbering note: the 'revenue shall not exceed funds required' language is actually Art. XIII D §6(b)(1), not (b)(2); §6(b)(3) is the parcel-level proportionality clause. This matters for how a complaint would plead the claim, though the substantive analysis is similar either way.

Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 is the closest direct precedent: it held tiered rates must be justified by actual marginal/incremental cost of service, and rejected 'conservation-incentive' pricing untethered from cost as a standalone justification. The district's own admission that $0.85 derives partly from incentive pricing (not strictly the 1.6x cost differential) is the strongest evidence for a violation — but only as to the increment above cost, not necessarily the whole surcharge.

No CA case squarely holds that funding conservation rebates/education from rate revenue is a per se or 'categorical' Prop 218 violation; turf/appliance rebate programs funded from tiered rates are common utility practice and are often defensible as system-wide demand-management costs (reduced peaking/capacity needs benefit all ratepayers, including payors), which cuts against a bright-line rule.

City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191 and Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 support a 'reasonable relationship'/no-mathematical-precision standard, giving agencies some latitude to pool revenue across an enterprise fund for legitimate service-related purposes without dollar-for-dollar payor traceability — suggesting courts may tolerate the 30% debt-service and 15% education allocations more readily than the 55% rebate allocation.

A court is more likely to sever the analysis (invalidating only the incentive-pricing markup or the rebate-heavy 55% tranche) than to adopt the sweeping 'categorically impermissible' framing in the sub-question itself; the doctrine is fact/cost-methodology-driven, not a per se rule, which should modestly lower confidence in any absolute answer either direction.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Expert/actuarial recalculation showing how much of the $0.85 rate is attributable to the 1.6x cost differential versus pure incentive pricing (a small markup favors the district; a large one favors plaintiffs); the rate study's own characterization of rebate/education/conservation program costs as part of 'cost of service' to high-tier users specifically (would reframe the allocation as internally cost-justified rather than cross-subsidized); and any post-Capistrano published appellate decisions specifically addressing rebate-funded tiered surcharges (e.g., subsequent water-rate Prop 218 litigation) that either extend or cabin Capistrano's reasoning to revenue-use questions rather than just rate-derivation questions.
